title: Isaiah Chapter 26
subtitle: A Song of Trust and Resurrection Hope
structureSubtitle: See perfect peace for those who trust

verses.text: In that day this song will be sung in the land of Judah: We have a strong city; God makes salvation its walls and ramparts.
Open the gates that the righteous nation may enter, the nation that keeps faith.
You will keep in perfect peace those whose minds are steadfast, because they trust in you.
Trust in the LORD forever, for the LORD, the LORD himself, is the Rock eternal.
He humbles those who dwell on high, he lays the lofty city low; he levels it to the ground and casts it down to the dust.
Feet trample it down— the feet of the oppressed, the footsteps of the poor.
The path of the righteous is level; you, the Upright One, make the way of the righteous smooth.
Yes, LORD, walking in the way of your laws, we wait for you; your name and renown are the desire of our hearts.
My soul yearns for you in the night; in the morning my spirit longs for you. When your judgments come upon the earth, the people of the world learn righteousness.
But when grace is shown to the wicked, they do not learn righteousness; even in a land of uprightness they go on doing evil and do not regard the majesty of the LORD.
LORD, your hand is lifted high, but they do not see it. Let them see your zeal for your people and be put to shame; let the fire reserved for your enemies consume them.
LORD, you establish peace for us; all that we have accomplished you have done for us.
LORD our God, other lords besides you have ruled over us, but your name alone do we honor.
They are now dead, they live no more; their spirits do not rise. You punished them and brought them to ruin; you wiped out all memory of them.
You have enlarged the nation, LORD; you have enlarged the nation. You have gained glory for yourself; you have extended all the borders of the land.
LORD, they came to you in their distress; when you disciplined them, they could barely whisper a prayer.
As a pregnant woman about to give birth writhes and cries out in her pain, so were we in your presence, LORD.
We were with child, we writhed in labor, but we gave birth to wind. We have not brought salvation to the earth, and the people of the world have not come to life.
But your dead will live, LORD; their bodies will rise— let those who dwell in the dust wake up and shout for joy— your dew is like the dew of the morning; the earth will give birth to her dead.
Go, my people, enter your rooms and shut the doors behind you; hide yourselves for a little while until his wrath has passed by.
See, the LORD is coming out of his dwelling to punish the people of the earth for their sins. The earth will disclose the blood shed on it; the earth will conceal its slain no longer.

1.seeing: The song opens with a declaration of security: God Himself is the city's defense. 'Salvation' becomes the very architecture—walls and ramparts—not human engineering but divine protection. This is the counterpoint to the 'lofty city' that will fall.
1.life: What walls are you trusting for protection? Career, relationships, finances? This verse reframes security entirely: only God's salvation provides real walls. Everything else is vulnerable to breach.
1.teach: The 'strong city' of God stands in contrast to every human fortress. Throughout Isaiah's apocalypse (chapters 24-27), earthly cities fall while God's city endures. True security is never self-made but God-given.

2.seeing: The gates open not for the powerful or wealthy but for 'the righteous nation... that keeps faith.' Entry is based on character and faithfulness, not status. The Hebrew for 'keeps faith' (shomer emunim) implies ongoing, habitual trust.
2.life: Are you keeping faith—consistently, habitually trusting? God's city has open gates, but the invitation is for those who persist in faithfulness. It's not about perfection but about direction—are you oriented toward trust?
2.teach: God's kingdom has an entry requirement: faith. Not achievement, not lineage, not religious performance—but steadfast trust. This foreshadows the New Testament principle that the righteous live by faith.

3.seeing: The Hebrew literally reads 'peace, peace'—doubled for emphasis and completeness. 'Steadfast mind' (yetzer samukh) means a mind that is fixed, supported, leaning on God. Peace isn't the absence of trouble but the presence of trust.
3.life: Where is your mind fixed right now? Anxiety and peace cannot coexist when your mind is anchored in God. This isn't willpower—it's the natural fruit of genuine trust. What would it look like to fix your thoughts on God today?
3.teach: This is one of Scripture's most beloved promises. The doubled 'peace' (shalom shalom) indicates complete, total peace—inner and outer, present and eternal. The condition isn't effort but orientation: a mind stayed on God.

4.seeing: 'Trust in the LORD forever' gives the response to verse 3's promise. The doubled divine name ('the LORD, the LORD') parallels the doubled 'peace' of verse 3. 'Rock eternal' (tsur olamim) means God's reliability spans all of time.
4.life: Is your trust temporary or forever? We often trust God in crisis and forget Him in comfort. This verse calls for permanent, habitual trust—not seasonal faith but lifelong reliance on the eternal Rock.
4.teach: The metaphor of Rock conveys stability, permanence, and shelter. Unlike shifting sand, the Rock eternal never erodes. Every other foundation eventually crumbles; God alone remains across all generations.

5.seeing: The 'lofty city' represents human pride and self-sufficiency—the opposite of trust. God doesn't just defeat it; He levels it completely: 'to the ground... to the dust.' The reversal is total and irreversible.
5.life: What 'lofty cities' in your life need to be brought low? Pride, self-reliance, and the illusion of control are all structures God must humble before trust can take root.
5.teach: Throughout Isaiah, God opposes the proud and exalts the humble. The lofty city's destruction isn't vindictive but necessary—pride must fall for God's kingdom to be established. Humility precedes true security.

6.seeing: The ultimate reversal: the oppressed and poor—those the lofty city once trampled—now walk over its ruins. God's justice inverts worldly hierarchies. The powerful become rubble beneath humble feet.
6.life: Have you experienced God lifting the lowly? This verse encourages anyone who feels crushed by powerful systems or people. God sees, and He will reverse the order. The last truly will be first.
6.teach: God's justice consistently favors the marginalized. The poor trampling the ruins of the proud city is a picture of eschatological reversal—the kingdom principle that runs from Hannah's song through Mary's Magnificat.

7.seeing: God is called 'the Upright One' (yashar)—His own character is the standard for the level path. He doesn't just command righteousness; He embodies it and then smooths the way for those who walk in it.
7.life: Does your path feel rough and uneven? This verse promises that God actively smooths the way for those committed to righteousness. The path may not be easy, but it is level—fair, just, and navigable.
7.teach: The 'level path' doesn't mean trouble-free but means morally clear and divinely guided. God removes the obstacles of confusion and moral ambiguity for those who pursue uprightness. His character guarantees their direction.

8.seeing: Waiting and desiring are the twin postures of the faithful. 'Walking in the way of your laws' isn't legalism but love—they desire God's name and renown above all else. Obedience flows from longing, not obligation.
8.life: Is God's name the 'desire of your heart'? This verse reveals that authentic obedience grows from genuine desire. When you truly want God's renown more than your own comfort, walking in His ways becomes natural.
8.teach: Biblical waiting isn't passive but active: it involves walking in God's laws while yearning for His presence. The desire for God's 'name and renown' shows that the righteous care more about God's glory than their own vindication.

9.seeing: Night and morning encompass all of life—the psalmist yearns for God around the clock. The second half reveals a surprising truth: God's judgments teach righteousness. What seems harsh produces learning.
9.life: Do you yearn for God in the night seasons—during insomnia, anxiety, loss? And do you recognize that God's judgments in the world actually teach people to live rightly? Difficulty can be a classroom.
9.teach: The day-and-night longing echoes Psalm 42's thirsting for God. The connection between judgment and learning righteousness shows that God's corrective actions have pedagogical purpose—they teach the world what's right.

10.seeing: This verse presents the tragic paradox: grace shown to the wicked doesn't produce repentance. Even surrounded by uprightness, they persist in evil. They 'do not regard the majesty of the LORD'—willful blindness to God's greatness.
10.life: Have you seen grace wasted? This verse is a warning: receiving God's kindness without responding in faith leads to hardening. Grace isn't automatic transformation—it requires a willing heart to produce change.
10.teach: Grace without response leads to presumption. Paul echoes this in Romans 2:4—God's kindness is meant to lead to repentance, but some despise it. The wicked's failure isn't God's inadequacy but their stubborn refusal to see.

11.seeing: God's hand is 'lifted high'—His power is on display—yet 'they do not see it.' This willful blindness parallels verse 10. The prayer shifts to petition: let them see, let them be ashamed, let fire consume.
11.life: Are you blind to what God is doing around you? Sometimes we're so absorbed in our own agenda that we miss God's outstretched hand. This verse challenges us to open our eyes to His activity.
11.teach: The lifted hand symbolizes divine power ready to act. The tragedy is that God's enemies refuse to see it. The prayer for fire reflects not vengeance but justice—the natural consequence of rejecting the God whose hand is raised in both mercy and judgment.

12.seeing: A stunning confession: 'All that we have accomplished you have done for us.' Every achievement, every victory, every good work—ultimately God's doing. Peace itself is something God 'establishes,' not something humans manufacture.
12.life: Can you honestly say 'all that I have accomplished, You have done for me'? This verse demolishes self-made pride. Your successes, your growth, your breakthroughs—they trace back to God's enabling hand.
12.teach: This is one of Scripture's clearest statements of divine sovereignty in human achievement. It parallels Paul's 'by the grace of God I am what I am' (1 Corinthians 15:10). True humility recognizes God behind every accomplishment.

13.seeing: The confession continues: 'other lords' have ruled—foreign powers, idols, false allegiances—but now loyalty belongs to God alone. 'Your name alone do we honor' marks a decisive break from divided loyalties.
13.life: What 'other lords' have ruled over you? Addiction, ambition, approval, anxiety? This verse models the prayer of renewed allegiance: acknowledging past bondage while declaring exclusive devotion to God.
13.teach: Israel's history of serving other gods and foreign masters becomes a confession here. The return to honoring God's name alone echoes the first commandment. Repentance means dethroning every rival lord.

14.seeing: The 'other lords' of verse 13 are now dead and gone—'they live no more; their spirits do not rise.' God has erased them from memory. This contrasts sharply with verse 19, where God's people WILL rise.
14.life: The things that once controlled you don't have to define your future. Like these dead lords, past bondages can be wiped out by God's power. What former 'lords' do you need God to bury permanently?
14.teach: The contrast between verse 14 (the dead who don't rise) and verse 19 (the dead who do rise) is theologically crucial. Oppressors face permanent death; God's people face resurrection. Justice and hope meet in this distinction.

15.seeing: God has 'enlarged the nation' and 'extended all the borders'—the opposite of exile and reduction. Repetition ('enlarged... enlarged') emphasizes the magnitude of God's expansion. He gains glory through His people's flourishing.
15.life: God wants to enlarge your life—not in material wealth but in spiritual capacity, influence for good, and depth of character. Where is God expanding your borders right now? Can you see His glory in your growth?
15.teach: National enlargement recalls the Abrahamic promise of a great nation. God gains glory not through conquest but through blessing His people and expanding their reach. Divine glory and human flourishing are connected, not competing.

16.seeing: The tone shifts to lament. In distress, they came to God—but their prayer was barely a whisper. Discipline had crushed them so thoroughly that they could hardly speak. This is the prayer of the desperate, not the eloquent.
16.life: Have you ever been so overwhelmed that you could barely whisper a prayer? God hears whispered prayers just as clearly as shouted ones. Desperation doesn't disqualify your prayer—it may actually deepen it.
16.teach: This verse shows that God's discipline reduces human self-sufficiency to the point of total dependence. The barely-whispered prayer is actually the most honest prayer: stripped of pretense, raw with need.

17.seeing: The birth-pain metaphor captures the intensity of suffering—real, physical, agonizing. 'In your presence, LORD' is key: the suffering happens before God, not away from Him. He witnesses every contraction of pain.
17.life: Suffering can feel like labor—purposeful pain that should produce something. But as verse 18 will reveal, human effort apart from God produces nothing. Are you laboring in your own strength or in God's presence?
17.teach: Birth-pain imagery appears throughout Scripture (Micah 4:9-10, John 16:21, Romans 8:22) to describe suffering that precedes deliverance. The pain is real but transitional—pointing toward something new.

18.seeing: The devastating punchline: all the labor produced nothing—'we gave birth to wind.' No salvation, no new life. Human effort without God is futile. This is the lowest point before the resurrection hope of verse 19.
18.life: Have you ever worked incredibly hard and produced nothing of lasting value? This verse validates that frustration. Apart from God's power, even our greatest efforts are 'wind.' The answer isn't to stop laboring but to labor with God.
18.teach: The futility of self-salvation is Isaiah's consistent message. Humanity cannot save itself—all its striving produces 'wind.' This sets up the dramatic reversal of verse 19, where God accomplishes what humans cannot: resurrection.

19.seeing: 'But your dead will live!'—the emphatic 'but' reverses everything. Where human effort failed (v18), God succeeds. 'Wake up and shout for joy, you who dwell in the dust'—this is one of the Old Testament's most explicit resurrection promises. God's dew brings life from death itself.
19.life: Whatever feels dead in your life—a dream, a relationship, your hope—God specializes in resurrection. This isn't wishful thinking; it's the character of the God who brings life from dust. Can you trust Him with your 'dead' places?
19.teach: This verse, alongside Daniel 12:2, forms the foundation of Old Testament resurrection hope. The 'dew of the morning' symbolizes God's life-giving power—gentle yet unstoppable. What humanity could not birth (v18), God raises from the grave.

20.seeing: After the resurrection promise comes a surprising command: hide. 'Enter your rooms and shut the doors'—echoing the Passover night when Israel sheltered while judgment passed (Exodus 12). Protection precedes final deliverance.
20.life: Sometimes God says 'wait and hide' before the breakthrough comes. This isn't cowardice but obedience. Are you willing to shelter in God's protection while His wrath deals with evil? Trust requires patience.
20.teach: The Passover echo is unmistakable: behind closed doors, God's people are safe while judgment falls. This 'little while' of hiding points to the New Testament concept of patient endurance before Christ's return (Hebrews 10:37).

21.seeing: The chapter's final verse reveals why hiding is necessary: the LORD is coming to judge. 'The earth will disclose the blood shed on it'—every injustice will be exposed. Nothing stays hidden; the earth itself testifies against violence.
21.life: Every act of injustice, every hidden wrong, will be brought to light. This is both sobering and comforting. If you've been wronged, God sees and will act. If you've wronged others, now is the time for repentance.
21.teach: The earth as witness to bloodshed echoes Genesis 4:10, where Abel's blood cried from the ground. God's final judgment is comprehensive—the very ground reveals what humanity tried to conceal. Justice is not just coming; it is certain.

from: Psalm 46:1 - God is our refuge and strength, an ever-present help in trouble
Psalm 48:1-3 - Great is the LORD and most worthy of praise in the city of our God
to: Hebrews 11:10 - He was looking forward to the city with foundations, whose architect and builder is God
Revelation 21:2 - I saw the Holy City, the new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God
1.context: The strong city whose walls are salvation anticipates the eternal city of God. From Zion to the New Jerusalem, God's dwelling place is always characterized by divine protection and salvation.

from: Psalm 118:19-20 - Open for me the gates of the righteous; I will enter and give thanks to the LORD
Psalm 24:7 - Lift up your heads, you gates; be lifted up, you ancient doors
to: Revelation 22:14 - Blessed are those who wash their robes, that they may go through the gates into the city
John 10:9 - I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved
2.context: Gates that open for the faithful anticipate Jesus as the gate and the open gates of the New Jerusalem. Entry has always been by faith, not by human merit.

from: Psalm 119:165 - Great peace have those who love your law, and nothing can make them stumble
Numbers 6:24-26 - The LORD bless you and keep you; the LORD make his face shine on you and give you peace
to: Philippians 4:6-7 - The peace of God, which transcends all understanding, will guard your hearts and minds in Christ Jesus
John 14:27 - Peace I leave with you; my peace I give you. I do not give to you as the world gives
3.context: Isaiah's 'perfect peace' for the steadfast mind finds its fullest expression in the peace Jesus gives—a peace that doesn't depend on circumstances but on trust in God's character.

from: Deuteronomy 32:4 - He is the Rock, his works are perfect, and all his ways are just
Psalm 18:2 - The LORD is my rock, my fortress and my deliverer
to: Matthew 7:24-25 - Everyone who hears these words of mine and puts them into practice is like a wise man who built his house on the rock
1 Corinthians 10:4 - They drank from the spiritual rock that accompanied them, and that rock was Christ
4.context: The Rock eternal of Isaiah becomes the rock on which the wise man builds and ultimately Christ Himself. God's unchanging nature is the foundation for eternal trust.

from: Isaiah 2:11-12 - The eyes of the arrogant will be humbled and human pride brought low
Psalm 147:6 - The LORD sustains the humble but casts the wicked to the ground
to: Luke 1:51-52 - He has scattered those who are proud; He has brought down rulers from their thrones and lifted up the humble
James 4:6 - God opposes the proud but shows favor to the humble
5.context: God's opposition to human pride is a constant biblical theme. The lofty city laid low foreshadows Mary's Magnificat and James's warning: pride invites divine resistance.

from: 1 Samuel 2:8 - He raises the poor from the dust and lifts the needy from the ash heap
Psalm 113:7-8 - He raises the poor from the dust and lifts the needy from the ash heap
to: Luke 6:20-21 - Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God
Matthew 5:5 - Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth
6.context: The poor trampling the ruins of the proud is the Old Testament version of the Beatitudes. God's kingdom consistently elevates the lowly and humbles the powerful.

from: Proverbs 3:5-6 - Trust in the LORD with all your heart; he will make your paths straight
Psalm 27:11 - Teach me your way, LORD; lead me in a straight path
to: Hebrews 12:13 - Make level paths for your feet, so that the lame may not be disabled, but rather healed
John 14:6 - I am the way and the truth and the life
7.context: God as the one who levels the path for the righteous finds its ultimate fulfillment in Jesus, who is the Way itself. The level path becomes a person.

from: Psalm 63:1 - You, God, are my God, earnestly I seek you; I thirst for you
Psalm 27:4 - One thing I ask from the LORD, this only do I seek: to dwell in the house of the LORD
to: Romans 8:23-25 - We wait eagerly for our adoption to sonship, the redemption of our bodies
Philippians 3:10 - I want to know Christ—yes, to know the power of his resurrection
8.context: The desire for God's name and renown echoes through Scripture. Waiting for God while walking in His ways becomes the Christian posture of eager anticipation for Christ's return.

from: Psalm 42:1-2 - As the deer pants for streams of water, so my soul pants for you, my God
Psalm 119:147 - I rise before dawn and cry for help; I have put my hope in your word
to: Romans 1:18 - The wrath of God is being revealed from heaven against all the godlessness and wickedness of people
Revelation 15:4 - For your righteous acts have been revealed
9.context: Night-and-morning yearning for God reflects the deepest human longing. God's judgments serve a teaching function—they reveal righteousness to a world that has lost its moral compass.

from: Ecclesiastes 8:11 - When the sentence for a crime is not quickly carried out, people's hearts are filled with schemes to do wrong
Jeremiah 5:3 - You struck them, but they felt no pain; you crushed them, but they refused correction
to: Romans 2:4 - God's kindness is intended to lead you to repentance
2 Peter 3:9 - The Lord is patient with you, not wanting anyone to perish, but everyone to come to repentance
10.context: The tragic irony of grace rejected runs through Scripture. God's patience is meant to produce repentance, but some harden under kindness that should have softened them.

from: Isaiah 5:12 - They have no regard for the deeds of the LORD, no respect for the work of his hands
Psalm 28:5 - Because they have no regard for the deeds of the LORD, he will tear them down and never build them up again
to: 2 Thessalonians 1:7-8 - The Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven with his powerful angels in blazing fire
Hebrews 10:27 - A fearful expectation of judgment and of raging fire that will consume the enemies of God
11.context: Willful blindness to God's power invites judgment. The fire reserved for enemies is not arbitrary but the just consequence of refusing to see what God has made plain.

from: Psalm 44:3 - It was not by their sword that they won the land; it was your right hand, your arm, and the light of your face
Deuteronomy 8:17-18 - You may say, 'My power and the strength of my hands have produced this wealth.' But remember the LORD your God
to: 1 Corinthians 15:10 - By the grace of God I am what I am, and his grace to me was not without effect
Ephesians 2:10 - We are God's handiwork, created in Christ Jesus to do good works, which God prepared in advance for us to do
12.context: The confession that God accomplishes all things for us demolishes human boasting. Paul's testimony echoes Isaiah's: every achievement traces back to divine grace, not human effort.

from: Deuteronomy 6:13-14 - Fear the LORD your God, serve him only. Do not follow other gods
Judges 10:13-16 - You have forsaken me and served other gods; go and cry out to the gods you have chosen
to: Acts 4:12 - Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved
1 Timothy 2:5 - There is one God and one mediator between God and mankind, the man Christ Jesus
13.context: Israel's confession of having served other lords becomes the church's confession of Christ's exclusive lordship. The journey from idolatry to monotheistic devotion reaches its climax in the lordship of Jesus.

from: Psalm 9:5-6 - You have rebuked the nations and destroyed the wicked; you have blotted out their name for ever and ever
Exodus 15:6 - Your right hand, LORD, was majestic in power; your right hand, LORD, shattered the enemy
to: Revelation 20:14 - Then death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire
1 Corinthians 15:26 - The last enemy to be destroyed is death
14.context: The permanent death of oppressive lords contrasts with the resurrection of God's people in verse 19. Death is the final fate of those who oppose God, while life awaits those who trust Him.

from: Genesis 12:2 - I will make you into a great nation, and I will bless you
Deuteronomy 32:8 - When the Most High gave the nations their inheritance, when he divided all mankind
to: Ephesians 2:19 - You are no longer foreigners and strangers, but fellow citizens with God's people
Revelation 7:9 - A great multitude that no one could count, from every nation, tribe, people and language
15.context: God's enlarging of the nation ultimately expands beyond ethnic Israel to include all peoples. The borders extend to encompass every tribe and tongue in God's eternal kingdom.

from: Psalm 18:6 - In my distress I called to the LORD; I cried to my God for help
Hosea 5:15 - In their misery they will earnestly seek me
to: 2 Corinthians 12:9-10 - My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness
Hebrews 12:11 - No discipline seems pleasant at the time, but painful. Later on, however, it produces a harvest of righteousness
16.context: Distress that drives people to God is a recurring biblical pattern. The barely-whispered prayer in discipline echoes Paul's discovery that God's power shines through human weakness.

from: Micah 4:9-10 - Why do you now cry aloud—have you no king? Writhe in agony, Daughter Zion, like a woman in labor
Jeremiah 4:31 - I hear a cry as of a woman in labor, a groan as of one bearing her first child
to: John 16:21 - A woman giving birth has pain because her time has come; but when her baby is born she forgets the anguish
Romans 8:22 - We know that the whole creation has been groaning as in the pains of childbirth right up to the present time
17.context: Birth-pain imagery spans both Testaments. Isaiah's labor in God's presence becomes creation's groaning in Romans 8—all of creation straining toward the new birth God alone can accomplish.

from: Hosea 13:13 - Pains as of a woman in childbirth come to him, but he is a child without wisdom
Ecclesiastes 1:14 - I have seen all the things that are done under the sun; all of them are meaningless, a chasing after the wind
to: John 15:5 - Apart from me you can do nothing
Romans 7:18 - For I know that good itself does not dwell in me; I have the desire to do what is good, but I cannot carry it out
18.context: Giving birth to wind captures the futility of self-salvation. Jesus states the principle directly: apart from Him, we can do nothing. Human striving without God produces only emptiness.

from: Daniel 12:2 - Multitudes who sleep in the dust of the earth will awake: some to everlasting life, others to shame
Ezekiel 37:5 - I will make breath enter you, and you will come to life
to: 1 Corinthians 15:52-54 - The dead will be raised imperishable, and we will be changed
1 Thessalonians 4:16 - The Lord himself will come down from heaven, and the dead in Christ will rise first
19.context: Isaiah 26:19 is one of the Old Testament's clearest resurrection promises. The 'dew of the morning' brings life from death—a hope fulfilled when Christ rose and guaranteed resurrection for all who trust Him.

from: Exodus 12:22-23 - Not one of you shall go out of the door of your house until morning; the LORD will pass over the doorway
Psalm 27:5 - In the day of trouble he will keep me safe in his dwelling; he will hide me in the shelter of his sacred tent
to: Hebrews 10:37 - For in just a little while, He who is coming will come and will not delay
1 Thessalonians 5:9 - God did not appoint us to suffer wrath but to receive salvation through our Lord Jesus Christ
20.context: The command to hide echoes Passover night: sheltering behind closed doors while judgment passes. Christians are promised protection from God's wrath through Christ—our Passover Lamb.

from: Genesis 4:10 - Your brother's blood cries out to me from the ground
Job 16:18 - Earth, do not cover my blood; may my cry never be laid to rest
to: Revelation 6:10 - How long, Sovereign Lord, holy and true, until you judge the inhabitants of the earth and avenge our blood?
2 Thessalonians 1:6-7 - God is just: He will pay back trouble to those who trouble you and give relief to you who are troubled
21.context: From Abel's blood crying out to the martyrs under the altar in Revelation, God hears every cry of injustice. Isaiah's promise that the earth will 'disclose the blood shed on it' guarantees that no wrong will remain hidden.

theme: A hymn of trust in God's perfect peace for steadfast minds, contrasting human futility with divine resurrection power

1.name: The Strong City
1.transition: Open the gates for the righteous nation—trust in the LORD forever, the Rock eternal

2.name: The Lofty City Brought Low
2.transition: God humbles the proud and exalts the poor—the oppressed trample the ruins of the lofty

3.name: The Way of the Righteous
3.transition: God makes the path level for the upright, yet even with grace the wicked refuse to learn

4.name: The LORD Alone Acts
4.transition: Only God establishes peace—other lords have ruled, but His name alone endures

5.name: The Birth Pains
5.transition: Like a woman in labor, they writhed in agony but gave birth to wind—human effort cannot save

6.name: Resurrection and Hiding
6.transition: Your dead will live! Their bodies will rise—then hide until God's wrath has passed

hinge.explanation: Theological Center (v3) — Perfect peace for minds stayed on God: the foundation of all trust

climax.explanation: Climactic Hope (v19) — 'Your dead will live!'—one of the OT's clearest resurrection promises
